title: Walmart facility closing in Illinois, impacting more than 100 workers
detail:
Walmart facility closing in Illinois, impacting more than 100 workers
Walmart facility closing in Illinois, impacting more than 100 workers · Journal Star
Hannah Hudnall and Mike Kramer, Peoria Journal Star
Fri, April 3, 2026 at 8:41 AM CDT 2 min read
WMT

Retail giant Walmart Inc. announced plans to close a facility in northern Illinois, impacting more than 100 employees.

The company notified the state about the closure on March 27 as part of the Worker Adjustment and Retraining Act, also known as WARN. Here's what we know.

What is the WARN Act?

Under Illinois law, employers must notify the state when they plan to lay off 75 or more full-time workers. The Act requires employers to provide at least 60 days advance notice of pending plant closures or mass layoffs.

The state issues monthly WARN reports, which include information about affected employers and their layoffs or plant closures.

Where is the Walmart facility located?

The impacted facility is located in northern Illinois, at 21430 South Cicero Avenue in Matteson.

Why is Matteson Walmart facility closing?

A Walmart spokesperson told the Peoria Journal the company is relocating operations from the Matteson facility to other locations within its network, citing changing customer needs.

How many Walmart employees are being laid off?

The facility closure will impact 111 employees, according to the company's WARN report.

What was in Walmart employee notice?

The company told NBC Chicago it notified employees about the facility closure in February.

Affected employees will reportedly have the opportunity to transfer to nearby facilities or other Walmart locations nationwide.

Associates moving to select facilities could be eligible for $7,500 transfer bonuses, relocation assistance and on-the-job training using advanced fulfillment technology.

"We’re grateful for everything our associates do every day," a spokesperson told the Peoria Journal Star. "And we’re committed to working one-on-one with each person to find the best path forward so they can remain part of the Walmart family."

Have layoffs started at Matteson Walmart?

According to the WARN report, layoffs at the facility started Sunday, March 29.

Business in Illinois: Wisconsin manufacturer and East Peoria company reach agreement on acquisition

Southwest Airlines, T-Mobile, more report upcoming layoffs across Illinois

Recent WARN reports show similar upcoming layoffs across northern Illinois.

Examples include Southwest Airlines Co., which is set to lay off 107 employees at O'Hare Airport starting June 4, and a T-Mobile facility in Schaumburg, which started letting 172 workers go April 2.

Upcoming layoffs were also reported by Trinity Christian College in Palos Heights, Illinois Central School Bus in Wilmington and Millwood Inc., in Melrose Park.

This article originally appeared on Journal Star: Walmart facility in Matteson to close. What the company said about layoffs

title: Walmart shoppers aren't breaking under the weight of $4 gas (yet)
detail:
Walmart shoppers aren't breaking under the weight of $4 gas (yet)
Brian Sozzi · Executive Editor
Updated Thu, April 2, 2026 at 7:31 AM CDT 3 min read
WMT

The cost-conscious Walmart (WMT) shopper doesn't appear to be breaking under the weight of $4 gas amid the US war on Iran.

How much longer that resilience can continue is anyone's guess.

"Management reiterated that there are clear psychological thresholds [for gas] ($3-4 manageable, $4-5 economic pressure, $5 shock)," Jefferies analyst Corey Tarlowe wrote in a note on Thursday after meeting with Walmart execs. "Importantly, they have not seen behavior changes in performance data, only in sentiment surveys. Current trends show no meaningful shift in baskets, trips, or mix."

More from Yahoo Scout

What psychological thresholds does Walmart track for gas prices?

How is Walmart managing inflation and cost pressures?

Why has Walmart stock outperformed during recent market decline?

How are rising gas prices affecting Walmart shoppers?

Walmart's cost of goods sold line appears to be holding up as well, based on Tarlowe's analysis.

Tarlowe noted that management acknowledged some exposure to high diesel prices in Walmart's private fleet, but that was baked into the company's guidance. Walmart's food inflation assumptions were also unchanged, he said, and the retailer said it can absorb around 50 to 100 basis points of price escalation through scale, sourcing, and pricing discipline.

In each area, duration is "the key risk variable," Tarlowe said.

A man rides his bike past Walmart store signage on April 23, 2012, in Mexico City, Mexico. (Daniel Aguilar/Getty Images) · Daniel Aguilar via Getty Images

Since the start of the war in Iran in late February, US gasoline prices have surged sharply, tracking the spike in global oil markets and reflecting one of the fastest increases in years.

National average gas prices have climbed from roughly the low-$3 range to above $4 per gallon in a matter of weeks, with some regions, such as California, seeing $6 a gallon or higher.

Read more: What an extended war with Iran could mean for gas prices

The jump has been driven primarily by supply disruptions tied to the Strait of Hormuz, a critical artery for global oil flows, along with higher shipping and insurance costs and a broader geopolitical risk premium.

As a result, US drivers are facing a sudden and highly visible increase in fuel costs, with analysts warning that prices could remain elevated even if tensions ease due to lingering supply constraints.

The economic uncertainty has begun to show up in economic data releases.

The University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index plummeted to 58.4 in late March — a level not seen since the height of the 2008 financial crisis. Real gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecasts for 2026 have already been trimmed by 40 basis points, a sharper downward revision than that seen in the Chinese economy.

The labor market is beginning to cool, with initial jobless claims ticking up to 235,000 last week as high-input costs force energy-sensitive industries to pause hiring.
